entry.title: How to Implement SAP Change Document
entry.content:
Introduction:

If you have ever been asked the question — who changed that field, and when — you will immediately appreciate why change documents matter. In any serious SAP implementation, traceability is not a nice-to-have. It is a business requirement, often driven by audit, compliance, or simply the need to investigate data quality issues after the fact.

SAP provides a standard, reliable framework for exactly this purpose: Change Documents. Once set up, every create, update, and delete operation on your custom tables produces a detailed, timestamped record of exactly what changed, written automatically to two standard SAP tables — CDHDR and CDPOS. No custom logging table, no triggers, no background jobs. The framework handles it all.

In this blog series, we will walk through a complete implementation from scratch. We will use a realistic example — a customer master with header information and associated bank accounts — to show how change documents work in practice, and how to build a clean, reusable ABAP class that wraps both the database operations and the change document recording in a single, easy-to-call interface.

What are Change Documents?

Change Documents are SAP’s built-in audit trail mechanism for custom business objects. When correctly implemented, every time a record in your custom table is created, modified, or deleted, SAP automatically captures:

  • Who made the change — the SAP user name
  • When the change was made — date and time
  • Which transaction or program triggered the change
  • Which fields changed — at field level
  • What the old value was
  • What the new value is

This information is stored across two standard SAP tables that you never need to create or manage yourself.

How does the Framework Work?

The change document framework does not intercept your database calls automatically. You have to call it explicitly. The framework works as follows:
Everything starts with the creation of the Change Document Object. This can be done in ADT or in transaction SCDO. Here, you define a named Change Document Object and assign your custom tables to it. Whether the field change will be tracked or not depends on the flag set in the Data Element. In ADT, as soon as you activate the object, it generates a class with a method WRITE. If you are using SAP GUI transaction(SCDO), you have the option to generate either a class or a function module.

Your Responsibility

The generated class method (WRITE) does one thing only — it compares the old and new versions of your data that you pass to it, and writes the differences to CDHDR and CDPOS. It does not touch your Z tables at all. That means your code is responsible for two things in the correct order:

  1. Write to your Z tables using INSERT, MODIFY, or DELETE
  2. Call the generated class method/FM, passing it both the old and new versions of the data

In the case of a Managed RAP application, the call is handled automatically by the framework. We do have to specify the Change Document Object in the Behaviour Definition.

Both operations must happen within the same Logical Unit of Work, so they commit together. If the database update succeeds but the FM call fails, you want a rollback – not a situation where your data changed, but no change document was recorded.

This is the most important point to understand. The method cannot read old values from the database itself — by the time you call it, your MODIFY statement may have already overwritten the old data. You must read the old values from the database before making any changes, hold them in memory, and pass them to the FM alongside the new values.

In the case of a Managed RAP application, the call is handled automatically by the framework. We do have to specify the Change Document Object in the Behaviour Definition.

Tables

We are going to work with two tables ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK. This is a typical header item table. I am going to work with UUID as key as I will extend these to the RAP application later. I have used SAP Data Elements for fields. These Data Elements for fields NAME, STREET, CITY, POSTAL_CODE, COUNTRY etc. have ‘Change Document Logging’ checked.

@EndUserText.label : 'Customer'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zzcustomer {
 
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key customer_id       : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  name                  : ad_name1;
  street                : ad_street;
  city                  : ad_city1;
  postal_code           : ad_pstcd1;
  country               : land1;
  local_created_by      : abp_creation_user;
  local_created_at      : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_by : abp_locinst_lastchange_user;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
}
@EndUserText.label : 'Customer Bank Details'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zzcustomer_bank {
 
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key bankid            : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  customer_id           : sysuuid_x16;
  bank_country          : banks not null;
  bank_key              : bankk not null;
  bank_account          : bankn;
  currency              : waers;
  local_created_by      : abp_creation_user;
  local_created_at      : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_by : abp_locinst_lastchange_user;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Change Document Object

Create Change Document Object in ADT or in transaction SCDO (I’ve used ADT). Add tables ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK. Check ‘Log Multiple Changes’ in both tables. Activate. You will notice it will create a class (ZCL_ZZCUSTOMER_CHDO) and show it just under the Change Document.

Wrapper Class

This class acts as a combined repository and audit layer — all database INSERT, MODIFY, DELETE operations on ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK go through this class, which also records the change document automatically. The caller simply calls the method and then COMMIT WORK.

Definition

CLASS zcl_customer_change_wrapper DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .
 
  PUBLIC SECTION.
 
    METHODS create
      IMPORTING
        is_hdr TYPE zzcustomer
        it_dtl TYPE zzcustomerbank_t
      RAISING
        cx_sy_open_sql_error.
 
    METHODS update
      IMPORTING
        is_hdr TYPE zzcustomer
        it_dtl TYPE zzcustomerbank_t
      RAISING
        cx_sy_open_sql_error.
 
    METHODS delete
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
      RAISING
        cx_sy_open_sql_error.
 
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
 
    METHODS call_write_document
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id          TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
        object_change_indicator TYPE if_chdo_object_tools_rel=>ty_cdchngindh
        it_hdr_new              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer
        it_hdr_old              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer OPTIONAL
        it_dtl_new              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank
        it_dtl_old              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank OPTIONAL.
 
 
    METHODS read_current_customer
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rs_hdr)  TYPE zzcustomer.
 
    METHODS read_current_customer_bank
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rt_dtl)  TYPE zzcustomerbank_t.
ENDCLASS.

Class Implementation

Method CREATE

METHOD create.
  SELECT SINGLE customer_id FROM zzcustomer
      WHERE customer_id = @IS_hdr-customer_id
      INTO @DATA(lv_exists).
 
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_sy_open_sql_error.
  ENDIF.
 
  DATA it_hdr_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
 
  APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_hdr_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_hdr>).
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING is_hdr TO <chgdoc_hdr> .
  <chgdoc_hdr>-kz = 'I' .
 
  LOOP AT it_dtl ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_record>) .
    APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_dtl_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_dtl>) .
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING <new_record> TO <chgdoc_dtl> .
    <chgdoc_dtl>-kz = 'I' .
  ENDLOOP.
 
 
  INSERT zzcustomer FROM @IS_hdr.
  INSERT zzcustomer_bank FROM TABLE @IT_dtl.
 
  " Change doc
  call_write_document(
    object_change_indicator = 'U'
    iv_customer_id          = is_hdr-customer_id
    it_hdr_new              = it_hdr_new
    it_dtl_new              = it_dtl_new ).
 
ENDMETHOD.

The object_change_indicator was passed value ‘U’ instead of ‘I’ to align with how the change document works in RAP.

Method UPDATE

METHOD update.
 
  " Read old state from DB before modifying
  DATA(ls_hdr_old) = read_current_customer( is_hdr-customer_id ).
  DATA(lt_dtl_old) = read_current_customer_bank( is_hdr-customer_id ).
 
  DATA it_hdr_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
  DATA it_hdr_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
 
  APPEND ls_hdr_old TO it_hdr_old .
  APPEND LINES OF lt_dtl_old TO it_dtl_old .
 
  APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_hdr_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_hdr>).
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING is_hdr TO <chgdoc_hdr> .
  <chgdoc_hdr>-kz = 'U' .
  <chgdoc_hdr>-client = sy-mandt .
 
  LOOP AT it_dtl ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_record>) .
    APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_dtl_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_dtl>) .
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING <new_record> TO <chgdoc_dtl> .
    <chgdoc_dtl>-kz = 'U' .
    <chgdoc_dtl>-client = sy-mandt .
  ENDLOOP.
 
  MODIFY zzcustomer FROM @IS_hdr.
  MODIFY zzcustomer_bank FROM TABLE @IT_dtl.
 
  " Change doc
  call_write_document(
    object_change_indicator = 'U'
    iv_customer_id          = is_hdr-customer_id
    it_hdr_new              = it_hdr_new
    it_hdr_old              = it_hdr_old
    it_dtl_new              = it_dtl_new
    it_dtl_old              = it_dtl_old  ).
 
ENDMETHOD.

Method DELETE

METHOD delete.
 
  "Read old state from DB before modifying
  DATA(ls_hdr_old) = read_current_customer( iv_customer_id ).
  DATA(lt_dtl_old) = read_current_customer_bank( iv_customer_id ).
 
  DATA it_hdr_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
  DATA it_hdr_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
 
  APPEND ls_hdr_old TO it_hdr_old .
  APPEND LINES OF lt_dtl_old TO it_dtl_old .
 
  "Delete detail first, then header (referential integrity)
  DELETE FROM zzcustomer WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id.
  DELETE FROM zzcustomer_bank WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id.
 
  " Change doc
  call_write_document(
    object_change_indicator = 'D'
    iv_customer_id          = iv_customer_id
    it_hdr_new              = it_hdr_new
    it_hdr_old              = it_hdr_old
    it_dtl_new              = it_dtl_new
    it_dtl_old              = it_dtl_old  ).
 
ENDMETHOD.

Private helpera methods CALL_WRITE_DOCUMENT, READ_CURRENT_CUSTOMER, READ_CURRENT_CUSTOMER_BANK

METHOD call_write_document.
  TRY .
      zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>write(
        EXPORTING
                      objectid                = CONV cdobjectv( iv_customer_id )
                      tcode                   = sy-tcode
                      utime                   = sy-uzeit
                      udate                   = sy-datum
                      username                = sy-uname
                      object_change_indicator = object_change_indicator
                      xzzcustomer             = it_hdr_new
                      yzzcustomer             = it_hdr_old
                      upd_zzcustomer          = 'U'
                      xzzcustomer_bank        = it_dtl_new
                      yzzcustomer_bank        = it_dtl_old
                      upd_zzcustomer_bank     = 'U'
        IMPORTING
                      changenumber            = DATA(change_number)
      ).
 
    CATCH cx_chdo_write_error.
  ENDTRY.
ENDMETHOD.
 
 
METHOD read_current_customer_bank.
  SELECT * FROM zzcustomer_bank
    WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id
    INTO TABLE @RT_dtl.
ENDMETHOD.
 
METHOD read_current_customer.
  SELECT SINGLE * FROM zzcustomer
    WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id
    INTO @RS_hdr.
ENDMETHOD.

Usage Example

Testing: Create a New Customer

DATA: current_time TYPE timestampl,
      customer_id  TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id,
      bank_id      TYPE zzcustomer_bank-bankid.
 
GET TIME STAMP FIELD current_time.
"Get UUIDs for keys
customer_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
bank_id     = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
 
DATA(lo_cust) = NEW zcl_customer_change_wrapper( ).
 
lo_cust->create(
            is_hdr = VALUE zzcustomer(
                              customer_id           = customer_id
                              name                  = 'ABC Company'
                              street                = 'Long Street'
                              city                  = 'London'
                              postal_code           = 'EC10 5GH'
                              country               = 'GB'
                              local_created_by      = sy-uname
                              local_created_at      = current_time
                              local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                              local_last_changed_at = current_time
                              last_changed_at       = current_time
                           )
            it_dtl = VALUE zzcustomerbank_t(
                                ( bankid = bank_id
                                  customer_id           = customer_id
                                  bank_country          = 'GB'
                                  bank_key              = '123456'
                                  bank_account          = '123456789'
                                  currency              = 'GBP'
                                  local_created_by      = sy-uname
                                  local_created_at      = current_time
                                  local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                                  local_last_changed_at = current_time
                                  last_changed_at       = current_time
                            )
         )
  ).
 
COMMIT WORK.

Check table ZZCUSTOMER, ZZCUSTOMER_BANK, CDHDR and CDPOS.

Testing: Update Customer

DATA: current_time TYPE timestampl.
 
GET TIME STAMP FIELD current_time.
 
DATA(lo_cust) = NEW zcl_customer_change_wrapper( ).
 
  "Get customer_id and bank_id UUIDs from table ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK
lo_cust->update(
        is_hdr = VALUE zzcustomer(
                          customer_id = '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10D978'
                          name                  = 'XYZ Company'        "<< Change
                          street                = 'New Long Street'    "<< Change
                          city                  = 'London'
                          postal_code           = 'EC10 5GH'
                          country               = 'GB'
                          local_created_by      = sy-uname
                          local_created_at      = current_time
                          local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                          local_last_changed_at = current_time
                          last_changed_at       = current_time
                        )
        it_dtl = VALUE #(
                            ( bankid                = '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10F978'
                              customer_id           = '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10D978'
                              bank_country          = 'GB'
                              bank_key              = '30-40-50'   "<< Change
                              bank_account          = '123456789'
                              currency              = 'GBP'
                              local_created_by      = sy-uname
                              local_created_at      = current_time
                              local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                              local_last_changed_at = current_time
                              last_changed_at       = current_time
                            )
                         )
 ).
 
COMMIT WORK.

Check table ZZCUSTOMER, ZZCUSTOMER_BANK, CDHDR and CDPOS.

Testing: Delete Customer

DATA(lo_cust) = NEW zcl_customer_change_wrapper( ).
 
"Get customer_id UUID from table ZZCUSTOMER
lo_cust->delete( iv_customer_id =  '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10D978' ) .
COMMIT WORK.

Check table ZZCUSTOMER, ZZCUSTOMER_BANK, CDHDR and CDPOS.

Conclusion

Implementing a reliable audit trail doesn't require complex custom logging logic or performance-heavy database triggers. As shown in this guide, leveraging SAP’s standard Change Document Framework (SCDO) gives you a robust, compliant, and optimized solution right out of the box.

By wrapping both the database persistence layers (INSERT, MODIFY, DELETE) and the tracking mechanisms inside a single ABAP wrapper class, we successfully encapsulate the business logic. This pattern ensures that:

  • Data consistency is maintained via atomic operations (both the Z-tables and the CD tables update within the same Logical Unit of Work).

  • Code reusability is enhanced, hiding the boilerplate logic of tracking old vs. new memory states from caller programs.

  • Future readiness is built-in, paving a clean path if you decide to extend these custom tables into a Managed RAP (RESTful Application Programming) architecture down the line.

With CDHDR and CDPOS tracking your field-level changes seamlessly behind the scenes, answering "who changed what, and when" becomes a trivial task rather than a development headache.

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026 - Wave 2
entry.content:
Hi everyone, on behalf of the CX Product Success team at SAP, I'm delighted to share with you our latest deep dive videos as part of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026. 

Wave #2 (May-June 2026)

 
1. Innovate and Grow your Business with SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition
 
 

Join Daniela Stehle (Solution Value Advisor for Customer Experience at SAP) in an insightful discussion with Lucas Timm from SAP Product Management about the next evolution of digital commerce. Watch this product inspiration video to learn why SAP launched SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition and how it enables fast time‑to‑value for companies looking to quickly establish or expand their digital sales channels.
Discover the key differentiators of this integrated solution, including seamless end‑to‑end processes between commerce and SAP S/4HANA Cloud, reduced complexity, and a business‑user‑friendly experience. Learn how customers benefit from faster innovation, lower total cost of ownership, and scalable growth—while SAP manages the technical foundation.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:49 - Why did SAP release this new SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition?
01:38 - What are the key differentiators of this new solution?
02:39 - Which benefits do Commerce key users gain?
03:05 - What’s the business value for existing SAP customers?
05:12 - Start of the Demo
05:25 - Demo: Consumer-grade B2B experiences
06:37 - Demo: Activate your storefront from ERP data
07:48 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

2. Connected E-Commerce across SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition

Join Daniela Stehle (Solution Value Advisor for Customer Experience at SAP) in an insightful discussion with Lucas Timm from SAP Product Management about the next step in connected enterprise commerce. Watch this product inspiration video to explore how SAP‑managed integration seamlessly connects SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition—eliminating complexity and enabling truly unified end‑to‑end business processes.
Learn why integration has been a long‑standing challenge in commerce landscapes and how SAP’s managed approach removes the need for custom middleware, allowing customers to focus on selling, order management, and delivering great experiences while SAP handles connectivity in the background.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Start
00:26 - Introduction
00:49 - Why is integration so important between E-Commerce and ERP?
01:34 - How is SAP-managed integration different from customer-managed integration?
02:31 - What does SAP-managed integration mean for SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition?
03:14 - What’s the business value of SAP-managed integration for SAP Commerce Cloud?
04:09 - Start of the Demo

10:12 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Product Enrichment using Business AI
12:21 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Catalog Management & Assortment
13:41 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Storefront - Experience Shopping
14:14 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

3. SAP Engagement Cloud: AI Innovations and Google Cloud Partnership across the SAP Autonomous Suite

Join Nisha and Valentin from SAP for a strategic look at how SAP Engagement Cloud is redefining customer engagement in the era of Agentic AI and the SAP Autonomous Suite.
Discover how SAP Engagement Cloud extends beyond traditional marketing to transform enterprise-wide business signals into contextual, relevant, and timely customer interactions. Learn how SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Joule, and the expanded partnership with Google Cloud and Gemini Enterprise combine to create a powerful multi-agent framework that helps organizations deliver more intelligent and personalized engagement across marketing, commerce, sales, service, and operations.
In the inspiration demo, see how AI assistants and autonomous agents help marketers build data-driven campaigns, personalize content, optimize customer journeys, and make real-time decisions based on inventory, customer behavior, geographic data, and business signals. Learn how SAP Engagement Cloud enables organizations to move from isolated marketing activities to business-scale engagement, delivering relevance, agility, and competitive differentiation across the entire customer lifecycle.

The key highlights shared in this video are:

entry.title: Re: **Title:** [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Is there an...
entry.content:
Hi Byd,

Short answer: No — as of the current release (2602/2608 docs) there is no standard released API to post an intracompany asset transfer (ABUMN equivalent) in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. The released Asset Accounting posting APIs cover only acquisition, retirement, and revaluation, and the transfer logic cannot be reproduced through the Journal Entry API. I have addressed similar question a few days back; check it here.

1. Is there a standard released API (OData/SOAP) to post an intracompany asset transfer (ABUMN equivalent)?

No.

The complete list of released Asset Accounting APIs contains only Fixed Asset – Master Data, Post Asset Acquisition, Post Asset Retirement, and Post Asset Revaluation. There is no "Post Asset Transfer" service. Reference: APIs for Asset Accounting (SAP Help)

In the Acquisition API: Post Asset Acquisition supports only business transaction types RA10/RA11 (acquisition), RA50 (post-capitalization), RA13 (credit memo), RA12 (quantity adjustment) — no transfer. Reference: Fixed Asset – Post Asset Acquisition

In the Retirement API: Post Asset Retirement supports only retirement with/without revenue — no transfer. Reference: Fixed Asset – Post Asset Retirement

2. Which API / communication scenario should I use?

None exists for a transfer.

There is no communication scenario that posts an asset-to-asset transfer. For the asset posting APIs that do exist, use SAP_COM_0A95 (OData V4) — SAP recommends it for new projects (OAuth 2.0, target-specific authorization). SAP_COM_0563 (the older SOAP scenario) is deprecated. Reference: Fixed Asset – Post Asset Acquisition (Communication Scenarios / Recommendation)

3. Sample payload for the transfer (6100000-1 → 83000000-1)?

Not possible.

Because no transfer API is released, there is no transfer payload to provide. Your example cannot be posted via a dedicated service today.

Replicating the transfer via the Journal Entry API — recommended approach?

Not recommended / not technically viable.

Constraint: In Public Cloud, asset reconciliation G/L accounts are set to automatic posting only. You cannot post to them with a manual journal entry (meaning Post General Journal Entry), and OAMK (temporarily allow manual posting) is not available in Public Cloud. So the Journal Entry – Post API cannot post to the asset subledger. Reference: SAP Note 3349022 – Manual posting on Assets Reconciliation Account in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Even setting that aside, the Journal Entry API posts at G/L level only — it does not drive the asset transaction-type logic that proportionally moves APC and accumulated depreciation across all depreciation areas. There is therefore no asset transaction type you can pass to the JE API to reproduce ABUMN.

Important — what to do instead:

  • Perform the intracompany transfer manually in the Fiori UI (the standard "transfer within company code" posting, the successor to ABUMN). No released API automates it today.
  • I do not recommend bypassing it by chaining Post Asset Retirement + Post Asset Acquisition. That is not equivalent: it triggers gain/loss on retirement, re-capitalizes at a new value instead of preserving proportional net book value, and breaks the transfer audit trail.
  • If automation is a firm requirement, raise the gap via SAP for Me → Customer Influence requesting a released "Post Asset Transfer" API.

Summary: There is no ABUMN-equivalent transfer API in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — the released asset posting APIs are acquisition, retirement, and revaluation only, and the Journal Entry API cannot substitute because it can't post to the asset subledger. Post the transfer manually in the UI, avoid the retirement+acquisition workaround, and file a Customer Influence request if you need a released API.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Is there an option to use the cdsview I_ACTUALPLANJOU...
entry.content:
Hi @rogerio_vsouza 

Since I_ActualPlanJournalEntryItem is not extraction enabled, we cannot bring that in datasphere.

Either for remote tables or replication flow it should be extraction enabled.

 

below is my proposal 

I_ActualPlanJournalEntryItem is built on top of views  P_ActualPlanJrnlEntryItm - P_ActualPlanJrnlEntryItm -> I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEM-

I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEM-> I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEMRAWDATA

Base view I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEMRAW data is extraction and delta enabled.

Create a replication flow with I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEMRAW, use Initial+delta option and target as datasphere, which will store the data in local table.
Create graphical view using the local table and implement the logic present in I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEM,    P_ActualPlanJrnlEntryItm and I_ActualPlanJournalEntryItem.

We have done similar implementation for a different cube

entry.title: Bank Statement Automated Processing Rule not working
entry.content:
Dear Experts

we are using the Public Cloud. When using automated rules for the processing of the electronic bank statement (camt.053), the system picks up the automated rule based on the regex condition but it does not clear the open item. 

In the note to payee we have a string MH00 followed by six digits, e.g. MH00234561. This 6-digit number corresponds with open AR items which have this number in the reference field (XBLNR). We have defined the regex MH00(\d{6}) and applied the interpretation algorithm 001 (BELNR/XBLNR) to look for open items. The system actually tries to apply the regex and succeeds to do so. Althoug the identifies the open item it does not clear the open item. Only when during the manual re-processing we put a space between MH00 and the 234561 does the system finds the open item. How can we automate the clearing in the public cloud? In ERP it was possible to create a search string that could change note to payee thereby including the space. Is there a possibility to have the public cloud system to isolate the six-digit number from the string beginning with MH00?

Regards

entry.title: Re: IHB100 and IHB200 - fields needs to be distribute thr...
entry.content:
Hello Ram,

This is a known limitation. The fields from the IHB100 role (CREDIT_TAX_CODE, CREDIT_TAX_TYPE, DEBIT_TAX_CODE, DEBIT_TAX_TYPE) and the IHB200 role (BANK_AREA) are stored in BUT000 but are not part of the standard structure MDG_BS_BP_S_EXTERN, nor of the underlying ABAP structure BUS_EI_EXTERN. This means they cannot be distributed via BUSINESSPARTNERSUITEBULKREPLIC/BusinessPartnerSUITEBulkReplicateRequest_In in standard.

There is currently no standard way to achieve this. As a custom workaround, you would need to:

 

  1. Extend the external structure BUS_EI_EXTERN to add the missing fields along with the SPROXY structure.
  2. Implement BAdIs like MDG_SE_BP_BULK_REPLRQ_OUT and MDG_SE_BP_BULK_REPLRQ_IN to map the incoming web service payload to those extended fields and persist the values to BUT000 via custom code.

This is not an officially recommended approach and carries the usual risks of custom extensions to standard structures.

We are aware of similar requests for other fields not exposed in the BP web service. If you would like this to be considered for a future release, we recommend submitting an influence request through the SAP Customer Influence portal (https://influence.sap.com).

Kind Regards,
Victor

entry.title: Re: Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere using T006 (LB to KG Example)
entry.content:
@GiriVenkataAnandaRaju_Alapati 

Use the CONVERT_UNIT function in SAP Datasphere to convert LB to KG. Make sure T006 and T006D are imported into your space, then set the source unit to LB and the target unit to KG.

entry.content:

entry.title: Project Billing: Request PROJ_BILLG_ACCOUNT_TYPE_CHECK Changes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction 

If you are working with project billing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and need to control how billing account types are validated, the PROJ_BILLG_ACCOUNT_TYPE_CHECK feature toggle is central to that behavior. However, if your system is on a version prior to 2608.1, you may have noticed that this toggle is not available for direct configuration. You are not alone — and there is a clear path to getting it changed. 

This post walks you through exactly how to request that change through SAP Support. 

 

Why You Cannot Change It Yourself 

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition versions prior to 2608.1, the PROJ_BILLG_ACCOUNT_TYPE_CHECK toggle is not exposed for customer self-service configuration. This is by design for those releases — any modification to its state requires a backend change carried out by SAP Support. The relevant support component is PPM-SCL-BIL-FIO. 

 

How to Request the Change 

Start by logging in to the SAP Support Portal and creating a new incident under component PPM-SCL-BIL-FIO. 

In the incident description, be specific about what you need. State clearly whether you want the toggle set to ON, which enables billing account type validation, or OFF, which disables it.  

Make sure you include all required fields — your System ID, tenant details, contact information, and a brief note on the business impact. Once the incident is submitted, monitor it for a confirmation from SAP Support that the change has been applied. 

 
Verifying the Change 

After receiving confirmation from SAP Support, log in to your system and verify that the toggle reflects the state you requested. Do not stop at verification alone — run a practical test in your project billing transactions to confirm that the billing account type validation behavior matches what you expect. This step ensures the system is functioning correctly end to end. 
 

 
What Changes in Version 2608.1 

It is worth noting that starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608.1, this toggle will be available for self-service configuration. If you are a customer planning to upgrade to 2608.1 or later, you will no longer need to raise an incident for this — you can manage it directly. If you are approaching that upgrade, factor this into your planning. 

Conclusion 

Managing feature toggles like PROJ_BILLG_ACCOUNT_TYPE_CHECK in earlier versions of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition requires routing the request through SAP Support via component PPM-SCL-BIL-FIO. The process is simple when you know the steps: raise an incident, specify the desired toggle state clearly, and verify the outcome once the change is confirmed. If you have questions or run into issues during this process, feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Modernizing SAP Journal Entry Integration: From SOAP to REST with API Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Standard SAP Journal Entry APIs are typically exposed as SOAP services. While they work well within SAP landscapes, they often introduce challenges when integrating with external systems, especially those expecting REST-based interfaces.

In a recent project, this approach was used to expose Journal Entry functionality to an external non-SAP application, where the SOAP interface introduced integration complexity and increased development effort.

Understanding the Standard Journal Entry API

SAP provides standard SOAP-based APIs for Journal Entry processing (such as JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In), enabling the creation of accounting documents in S/4HANA.

For reference, SAP official API documentation can be found here: Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous)

Example of SOAP payload structure

Why This Becomes a Challenge

In many integration scenarios, especially when exposing services to external systems or partners, SOAP-based interfaces introduce a few challenges:

  • Complex XML payload structure
  • Larger message sizes
  • Harder consumption for non-SAP systems
  • Higher processing and transformation effort

These aspects can directly impact both development effort and operational costs, particularly in high-volume scenarios.

A REST-Oriented Integration Approach

To address these challenges, an integration layer was introduced using SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration), enabling transformation from SOAP to REST.

High-level architecture: REST exposure over standard SAP SOAP APIs using Integration Suite

Example Request Transformation

The REST payload was designed using a simplified and standardized naming convention aligned with common SAP terminology:

{
  "journalEntry": {
    "documentReferenceID": "REF_001",
    "companyCode": "1000",
    "documentDate": "2026-01-15",
    "postingDate": "2026-01-15",
    "items": [
      {
        "glAccount": "400000",
        "amount": 150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 01",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "internalOrder": "900123",
          "costCenter": "CC1000"
        }
      },
      {
        "glAccount": "200000",
        "amount": -150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 02",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "profitCenter": "PC1000"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

The payload structure was simplified using standardized and generalized field naming to focus on readability and integration usability, without exposing real business data.

Below is an example of how the simplified REST payload is transformed into the SOAP structure required by the standard SAP API, highlighting the mapping between a consumer-friendly model and the SAP-specific format:

<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest xmlns:ns0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN">      
      <MessageHeader>
        <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
      </MessageHeader>
      <JournalEntryCreateRequest>
        <MessageHeader>
          <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
        </MessageHeader>
        <JournalEntry>
          <OriginalReferenceDocumentType>BKPFF</OriginalReferenceDocumentType>
          <BusinessTransactionType>RFBU</BusinessTransactionType>
          <AccountingDocumentType>SA</AccountingDocumentType>
          <DocumentReferenceID>REF_001</DocumentReferenceID>
          <CreatedByUser>API_USER</CreatedByUser>
          <CompanyCode>1000</CompanyCode>
          <DocumentDate>2026-01-15</DocumentDate>
          <PostingDate>2026-01-15</PostingDate>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>400000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 01</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <OrderID>900123</OrderID>
              <CostCenter>CC1000</CostCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>200000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">-150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 02</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <ProfitCenter>PC1000</ProfitCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
        </JournalEntry>
      </JournalEntryCreateRequest>
    </ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

The goal was to make the API easier to consume while preserving compatibility with the standard SAP interface.

Implementation in SAP Integration Suite

The transformation was implemented using standard Cloud Integration components such as Content Modifier and Message Mapping. In this specific scenario, Message Mapping was sufficient to handle the required transformations, without the need for additional Groovy scripting.

The integration flow was responsible for:

  • Receiving REST requests (JSON)
  • Transforming payloads into SOAP XML
  • Calling the standard SAP API
  • Handling responses and errors consistently

Special attention was required when handling nested structures such as account assignments, where JSON elements needed to be carefully mapped into the corresponding SOAP hierarchy.

Integration Flow

Error Handling

Error handling was a key aspect of the integration design, ensuring that technical SOAP faults were translated into clear and consistent REST responses for API consumers.

Different HTTP status codes (such as 400, 401, 403, and 500) were mapped based on the error context, providing more meaningful responses aligned with REST principles.

The following Groovy script was used within the integration flow to standardize error responses:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def status = message.getHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", Integer)
    def exceptionMessage = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")?.getMessage()    

    if (!status) {
        if (exceptionMessage?.contains("Validation")) {
            status = 400
        } else {
            status = 500
        }
    }

    message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", status)
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    def response

    switch (status.toString()) {
        case "400":
            response = """{"error":"BAD_REQUEST", "message":"Invalid payload","details":"${exceptionMessage ?: ""}"}"""
            break

        case "401":
            response = '{"error":"UNAUTHORIZED","message":"Authentication failure with backend system"}'
            break

        case "403":
            response = '{"error":"FORBIDDEN","message":"Access denied to backend system"}'
            break

        case "500":
            response = '{"error":"INTERNAL_ERROR","message":"Error processing request in backend system"}'
            break

        default:
            response = '{"error":"UNKNOWN_ERROR","message":"Unknown error"}'
    }

    message.setBody(response)

    return message;
}

In addition to technical error handling, business validation errors returned by the SAP backend were also mapped into structured JSON responses.

Example of business error response:

{
  "journalEntryResponse": {
    "documentInfo": {
      "accountingDocument": "0000000000",
      "companyCode": "1000",
      "fiscalYear": "2026"
    },
    "messages": [
      {
        "code": "RW609",
        "message": "Error in document: BKPFF (S4 system)"
      },
      {
        "code": "RW033",
        "message": "FI/CO interface: balance in transaction currency is not zero (BRL -0.01)"
      }
    ]
  }
}

This approach avoids exposing raw SOAP fault structures directly to consumers and provides a more consistent, readable, and predictable API behavior.

It also improves integration debugging by delivering structured and meaningful error messages that can be easily consumed by external systems.

Results and Benefits

This approach resulted in several improvements:

  • Easier consumption for external systems
  • Reduced payload size compared to standard SOAP structures
  • Better alignment with API-first and REST-first strategies
  • Improved maintainability and reusability

In addition, a simple comparison between SOAP and REST payloads highlighted a significant reduction in message size.

In this scenario, the SOAP payload was 2,454 bytes, while the equivalent REST payload was 740 bytes — representing a reduction of approximately 70% in message size.

SOAP vs REST payload size comparison

In high-volume scenarios, reducing message size can also contribute to lower processing and integration costs.

Design Considerations and Trade-offs

While simplifying the API, some trade-offs were considered. SOAP fault responses contain detailed technical information, which may not be fully exposed in REST responses.

This required balancing usability for external consumers with the need for sufficient diagnostic detail.

API Management Considerations

The API was exposed through SAP API Management to enable:

  • Security and authentication
  • Monitoring
  • Version control

API Management Overview

Final Thoughts

This was an interesting exercise in modernizing standard SAP APIs for broader consumption scenarios. The approach was successfully implemented in a real customer scenario, proving effective in improving integration usability.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling similar challenges.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas on similar integration scenarios.

entry.title: Extending the Fiori App Display Supplier List (F1861): A Practical Approach Using CDS Views
entry.content:
Introduction

Extending standard SAP Fiori applications is a common requirement in S/4HANA projects.

In a recent scenario, I needed to include additional supplier fields from table LFA1 into the standard app Display Supplier List (F1861). However, even though the fields existed in the backend, they were not available in the Fiori application.

The Challenge

At first glance, extending a standard app may seem straightforward, but when dealing with CDS-based applications, simply adding fields at database level is not enough.

The F1861 app relies on CDS views and requires specific extension steps to properly expose additional fields.

SAP Note

According to SAP Note 3200905, extending standard CDS-based applications requires following a specific approach to ensure that custom fields are correctly exposed in both UI and analytical layers.

3200905 - Display Supplier List: Standard extensibility delivered by View Entity

Approach Overview

To address this, the following approach was implemented:

  • Extend the CDS view used as data source (C_Supplier)
  • Extend the consumption CDS view (C_SupplierList)
  • Apply UI annotations via Metadata Extension

Implementation

The standard interface view C_Supplier was extended to include custom fields from table LFA1.

extend view C_Supplier with ZEXT_C_SUPPLIER
  association [1..1] to lfa1 as _Supplier on $projection.supplier = _Supplier.lifnr
{
  _Supplier.ort02                                         as ExtDistrictName,
  _Supplier.brsch                                         as ExtIndustry,
  cast( _Supplier.gbdat as zext_gbdat preserving type )   as ExtBirthDate, // Custom data element used – standard conversion exit PDATE is not supported in CDS
  _Supplier.txjcd                                         as ExtTaxJurisdiction,
  _Supplier.regss                                         as SupplIsSocialInsuranceRegtrd,
  _Supplier.actss                                         as SocialInsuranceActivityCode,
  _Supplier.cnae                                          as ExtTaxEconomicActivityCode
}

A custom data element was created for the birth date field, as the standard conversion exit (PDATE) is not supported in CDS views. This allowed proper handling of the field without relying on conversion exits.

Next, the consumption view C_SupplierList was extended:

extend view C_SupplierList with ZEXT_C_SUPPLIERLIST
{
  cast( '' as ort02_gp )                         as ExtDistrictName,
  cast( '' as brsch )                            as ExtIndustry,
  cast( '' as zext_gbdat )                       as ExtBirthDate,
  cast( '' as txjcd )                            as ExtTaxJurisdiction,
  cast( '' as j_1aregss preserving type )        as SupplIsSocialInsuranceRegtrd,
  cast( '' as j_1aactss )                        as SocialInsuranceActivityCode,
  cast( '' as j_1bcnae )                         as ExtTaxEconomicActivityCode
}

A metadata extension was then used to control the visibility of fields in the UI:

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
annotate view C_SupplierList with
{
  @UI: { hidden: true }
  BPTaxType;

  @UI: { hidden: true }
  VATRegistration;

  @UI: { hidden: true }
  TaxTypeName;
}

This approach ensures that the extension remains upgrade-safe and aligned with SAP extensibility guidelines.

Result

After the extensions and annotations, the custom fields were successfully displayed in the F1861 app.

Display Supplier List (new fields)

Challenges Faced

Some challenges were encountered during the implementation:

  • Fields not appearing due to missing annotations
  • Issues with CDS associations
  • Inconsistent behavior when extending standard views
  • Limitations when reusing associations locally

Key Learnings

This scenario highlights several important aspects when working with SAP extensibility:

  • Understanding the CDS view hierarchy is essential
  • Metadata Extensions are required for UI exposure
  • SAP Notes often contain critical implementation details

Final Thoughts

This scenario demonstrates how standard SAP applications can be extended in a structured way, without modifying the core system, following clean core principles.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling Fiori extensibility scenarios.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas.

entry.title: Re: Matchning incoming payment automatically without lead...
entry.content:
Good day @olivia_johansson 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your screenshots, the issue is that you are trying to select an interpretation algorithm in a place where the system expects a processing instruction.

In Manage Processing Rules for Bank Statements, the rule action Clear in Sequence uses processing instructions, not the full list of bank statement interpretation algorithms. Note that a processing instruction is connected to an allocation or interpretation algorithm. E.g., 0011 Reference linked to interpretation algorithm 421.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/4865ed7d26304aa1b011db7a52131bea.html?locale=en-US

So, in simple terms:

  1. You cannot freely select algorithm 026 in the processing rule screen unless it is an available processing instruction.
  2. Algorithm 026 belongs to the electronic bank statement interpretation logic. It is used as Search for reference document number with leading zeros, if < 10, where the system adds leading zeros up to ten digits and compares the reference document number. See Interpretation Algorithms for more details.
  3. Your example CINV 672026REPP versus 000000672026REPP is not only a simple missing leading zero case. It also contains a prefix and suffix, so algorithm 026 may not be sufficient unless the relevant reference part can be interpreted as expected.

Here is what I suggest you check:

  1. In Configuration, open Make Global Settings for Electronic Bank Statement and check the assignment of the external transaction type used in the bank statement item. Note that external transaction type triggers posting rules and interpretation algorithms during bank statement posting.                                                            

     

     

    See the following reference for more insights https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/9929e3049c88402a8d56e437a3967e38.html?locale=en-US

  2. Check whether the external transaction used on the bank statement item is really the same one where algorithm 026 was assigned.
  3. If you use Manage Processing Rules for Bank Statements, choose only one of the available processing instructions, for example 0011, but be aware that this uses algorithm 421, not algorithm 026.  See the following reference for more insights. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/4865ed7d26304aa1b011db7a52131bea.html?locale=en-US
  4. After you have activated the configuration, test it with a new bank statement item. If algorithm 026 is applicable, the system should find the open item from the reference number after adding the leading zeros. If the reference remains alphanumeric like CINV 672026REPP, align the open item reference with the bank reference or rais a case with SAP.

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Horizon Morning Becomes the New SAP Analytics Cloud Default Theme in Q4 2026
entry.content:
With the Q4 2026 release of SAP Analytics Cloud in November, we are excited to announce an important update in our user experience strategy: Horizon Morning will become the new default theme. For cusutomers on fast track tenants, the update will be available in early August already.

This change is part of our continued investment in aligning the interface of SAP Analytics Cloud with other SAP solutions, bringing a modernized visual language, improved accessibility, and enhanced usability to analytics users across the platform.

In addition to Horizon Morning, users also have access to a dark theme as well as high contrast theme and an automatic option that choses the theme closest to your OS setting. Find how to change the theme in the Horizon release blog.

Automatic Rollout for Users Without Custom Themes

With the Q4 2026 upgrade, all users who have not customized their theme either at the tenant level or user level will be automatically switched to the new Horizon Morning theme.

Users and tenants with custom theme selections will retain their current configuration. No changes will be made to override your customization.

This ensures a smooth and predictable transition while respecting existing preferences and branding setups.

Belize Remains Available (For Now)

Although Horizon Morning becomes the new default, the Belize theme will still be available in the theme selector. Users may switch back to Belize at any time if they prefer the familiar experience.

However, as part of our long‑term UI evolution, Belize is planned for deprecation in the coming quarters. Because of this, we encourage all users and administrators to begin transitioning to Horizon.

Why Move to Horizon Morning?

The Horizon design system brings several advantages:

  • A clean, contemporary visual aesthetic optimized for clarity
  • Improved contrast, spacing, and component behavior
  • Increased accessibility compliance
  • A consistent look and feel across the SAP product ecosystem

These enhancements ensure that users can work more efficiently and comfortably across scenarios.

entry.title: Upcoming Webinar: Onboarding Shippers to SAP Business Network for Logistics
entry.content:
Features in Focus webinars proudly presents a new webinar with the SAP Business Network for Logistics team with a focus on shipper onboarding for freight collaboration. Learn step-by-step how to create a subaccount in SAP Business Technology Platform, establish trust configurations, and subscribe to the required services to enable the shipper tenant. The session will also show how to access and begin using the shipper environment after setup. By the end, you will understand the prerequisites, including required permissions and account access, and be able to carry out the onboarding and configuration process confidently.

This session will be held on August 5, 2026. It is expected to last approximately 45 minutes and will also be recorded.

To secure your spot for this informative webinar, please visit the below registration link, according to your region. Once registered, and you are unable to attend, you will receive the recording of the webinar to enjoy in your own time. 

Choose the time that works best for you (same content in both sessions): 

Click here to register for Option 1 on August 5th 11:00am IST / 2:30pm JST / 3:30pm AEST

Click here to register for Option 2 on August 5th 11:00am EDT / 12:00pm BRT / 4:00pm BST

Who should attend?

  • Shippers

We look forward to your participation in this webinar!

Visit our Features in Focus Webinar calendar and Hub to stay up to date with all things related to our webinars and SBN!

entry.title: Re: Sending email attachment document based on selected l...
entry.content:
FORM send_mail.

  DATA: lt_binary      TYPE solix_tab,
        lv_xstring     TYPE xstring,
        lv_size        TYPE so_obj_len,
        lt_email       TYPE TABLE OF adr6-smtp_addr,
        lv_line        TYPE string,
        lv_email       TYPE adr6-smtp_addr,
        lt_excel       TYPE TABLE OF string,
        lv_string      TYPE string,
        lv_csv         TYPE string,
        lv_value       TYPE string,
        lv_header      TYPE string,
        
        lt_fieldcat    TYPE slis_t_fieldcat_alv,
        ls_fieldcat    TYPE slis_fieldcat_alv,

        lt_tvarvc      TYPE TABLE OF tvarvc,
        ls_tvarvc      TYPE tvarvc,
        lv_variant     TYPE disvariant-variant,

        lt_text TYPE soli_tab.



  FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lt_alv_data> TYPE STANDARD TABLE,
                 <ls_row>      TYPE any,
                 <lv_field>    TYPE any,
                 <fs_data>     TYPE any,
                 <fs_val>      TYPE any.

  DATA: ls_varkey TYPE ltdxkey,
      lt_fc_db  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ltdxdata. 


SELECT *
    INTO TABLE @LT_tvarvc
    FROM tvarvc
    WHERE name = 'Z_TVARVC_SET'.

  LOOP AT lt_tvarvc INTO ls_tvarvc.

    IF ls_tvarvc-low IS NOT INITIAL.
      APPEND ls_tvarvc-low TO lt_email.
    ENDIF.

    IF lv_variant IS INITIAL AND ls_tvarvc-high IS NOT INITIAL.
      lv_variant = ls_tvarvc-high.
    ENDIF.

  ENDLOOP.



  ls_varkey-report  = sy-repid.
  ls_varkey-variant = lv_variant.
  ls_varkey-type    = 'F'.
  ls_varkey-handle  = ''.


CALL FUNCTION 'LT_DBDATA_READ_FROM_LTDX'
  EXPORTING
    i_tool            = 'LT'
    is_varkey          = ls_varkey
  TABLES
     t_dbfieldcat      = lt_fc_db
  EXCEPTIONS
       OTHERS            = 7.


IF sy-subrc = 0.
   DELETE lt_fc_db WHERE param NE 'NO_OUT' AND param NE 'COL_POS'.

LOOP AT lt_fc_db INTO DATA(ls_filter) WHERE param = 'NO_OUT' AND value = 'X'.
  DELETE lt_fc_db WHERE key1 = ls_filter-key1. 
ENDLOOP.
DELETE lt_fc_db WHERE param = 'NO_OUT'.
SORT lt_fc_db BY value ASCENDING.
ENDIF.

CLEAR lv_header.
CLEAR ls_fieldcat.

  ls_fieldcat-fieldname = 'MATNR'.
  ls_fieldcat-seltext_m = 'Material'.
  APPEND ls_fieldcat TO lt_fieldcat.
 

  "add the field catalogue to populate the field names"

CLEAR lv_header.

LOOP AT lt_fc_db INTO DATA(ls_fc).

  READ TABLE lt_fieldcat INTO ls_fieldcat
       WITH KEY fieldname = ls_fc-key1.

  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    lv_value = ls_fieldcat-seltext_m.
  ELSE.
    lv_value = ls_fc-key1.
  ENDIF.

CONDENSE lv_value.

IF lv_header IS INITIAL.
  lv_header = lv_value.
ELSE.
  lv_header = |{ lv_header }{ cl_abap_char_utilities=>horizontal_tab }{ lv_value }|.
ENDIF.

ENDLOOP.

APPEND lv_header TO lt_excel.

LOOP AT it_data ASSIGNING <fs_data>.

  CLEAR lv_line.

  LOOP AT lt_fc_db INTO ls_fc.

  ASSIGN COMPONENT ls_fc-key1
  OF STRUCTURE <fs_data>
  TO <fs_val>.

CLEAR lv_value.

IF sy-subrc = 0.

  lv_value = |{ <fs_val> }|.

ENDIF.

IF lv_line IS INITIAL.
  lv_line = lv_value.
ELSE.
  CONCATENATE lv_line lv_value
    INTO lv_line
    SEPARATED BY cl_abap_char_utilities=>horizontal_tab.
ENDIF.


  ENDLOOP.

  APPEND lv_line TO lt_excel.

ENDLOOP.
 LOOP AT lt_excel INTO DATA(lv_temp).
    CONCATENATE lv_string lv_temp cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf
      INTO lv_string.
  ENDLOOP.

  CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_STRING_TO_XSTRING'
    EXPORTING text = lv_string
    IMPORTING buffer = lv_xstring.

  CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_XSTRING_TO_BINARY'
    EXPORTING buffer = lv_xstring
    TABLES binary_tab = lt_binary.

  lv_size = xstrlen( lv_xstring ).

  DATA: lo_send_request TYPE REF TO cl_bcs,
        lo_document     TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs,
        lo_recipient    TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs.

  lo_send_request = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).

lt_text = VALUE soli_tab(
  ( line = 'Subject: ' )
  ( line = '' )
  ( line = '                                          ' )
  ( line = '                MAIL BODY                 ' )
  ( line = '                                          ' )
  ( line = '' )
  ( line = 'Thank you.' )
).
  lo_document = cl_document_bcs=>create_document(
    i_type    = 'RAW'
    i_text    = lt_text
    i_subject = 'Your file name' ).

  lo_document->add_attachment(
    i_attachment_type    = 'XLS'
    i_attachment_subject = 'Report'
    i_attachment_size    = lv_size
    i_att_content_hex    = lt_binary ).

  lo_send_request->set_document( lo_document ).

  LOOP AT lt_email INTO lv_email.
    lo_recipient = cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address( lv_email ).
    lo_send_request->add_recipient( lo_recipient ).
  ENDLOOP.

  lo_send_request->send( ).
  COMMIT WORK.

  MESSAGE 'Process is Done' TYPE 'S'.
ENDFORM.

In my case, I had a similar requirement where the email attachment needed to contain only the columns from a saved ALV layout rather than all fields from the internal table.

Approach Used

  1. I maintained the recipient email addresses and ALV layout name in a TVARVC entry.

    • LOW = Email Address
    • HIGH = ALV Layout Variant Name (including variants beginning with /)

  2. During email generation, I read the TVARVC values:

    • Collected all email addresses into an internal table.
    • Retrieved the layout variant name.

 

SELECT *
INTO TABLE lt_tvarvc
FROM tvarvc
WHERE name = 'Z_TVARVC_SET'.

 

  1. To determine which columns are visible in the saved layout, I read the ALV layout definition from table LTDX using function module:

 

CALL FUNCTION 'LT_DBDATA_READ_FROM_LTDX'

 

with:

 

ls_varkey-report = sy-repid.
ls_varkey-variant = lv_variant.
ls_varkey-type = 'F'.

 

  1. The returned data contains ALV layout attributes such as:
    • NO_OUT (hidden fields)
    • COL_POS (column sequence)

I then:

  • Removed all hidden fields (NO_OUT = 'X').
  • Kept only visible columns.
  • Sorted by COL_POS to preserve the exact layout order chosen by the user.

 

DELETE lt_fc_db WHERE param NE 'NO_OUT'
AND param NE 'COL_POS'.
 
LOOP AT lt_fc_db INTO ls_filter
WHERE param = 'NO_OUT'
AND value = 'X'.
 
DELETE lt_fc_db WHERE key1 = ls_filter-key1.
ENDLOOP.
 
SORT lt_fc_db BY value ASCENDING.

 

  1. For the attachment header row, I matched the field names against the field catalog and used the column texts (SELTEXT_M) instead of technical names.

 

READ TABLE lt_fieldcat INTO ls_fieldcat
WITH KEY fieldname = ls_fc-key1.

 

  1. While building the attachment, I dynamically assigned only the fields contained in the selected layout:

 

ASSIGN COMPONENT ls_fc-key1
OF STRUCTURE <fs_data>
TO <fs_val>.

 

This ensured that:

  • Hidden columns were excluded.
  • Column order matched the ALV layout.
  • Header texts matched the ALV display.
  1. Finally, the generated tab-delimited file was converted to XSTRING/Binary and sent as an Excel attachment using BCS classes:

 

cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).
cl_document_bcs=>create_document( ).
lo_document->add_attachment( ).

 

Result

The emailed attachment always matches the saved ALV layout selected in TVARVC, including:

  • Visible columns only
  • User-defined column sequence
  • Correct header descriptions
  • Support for layout names containing /

This avoided maintaining a separate field list in the program and allowed business users to control the attachment structure simply by updating the ALV layout.

This solution worked well for background jobs where the attachment structure needed to be controlled without modifying ABAP code.
